metadata.title: Run /snickerdoodle: Audit and Close Your TODOs
metadata.playlist: Build the Recipe — Make Your Tool Conductor-Ready
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 5B brief — Nik's Recipe-Based Framework

beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam. Episode 5 of 6. The recipe from Episode 4 is a first draft. /snickerdoodle in Gru is the tool that turns it into a hardened, audited document with all unresolved items formally typed. The three-step workflow is: describe your pipeline to Gru, get a Claude Code agent prompt back, run that prompt in Claude Code to annotate the recipe, then bring the annotated recipe back to Gru and confirm it is ready. One note before we go further: the exact Gru command interface and /snickerdoodle behavior are course-internal — re-verify the current Gru reference before recording this video. This episode teaches the documented workflow from the assignment brief; if the commands have moved, reconcile before building. Let's go.

props.segment: Run /snickerdoodle: Audit and Close Your TODOs
props.command: 3-step audit: describe pipeline to Gru → /snickerdoodle → Claude Code annotates → /claude confirms.
props.runningText: re-verify Gru command reference before recording — course-internal tool…
output: Step 1: run /snickerdoodle in Gru with 3–5 sentence pipeline description
Step 2: paste output prompt into Claude Code — annotates recipe with typed TODOs
Step 3: run /claude in Gru when TODOs are closed — confirms recipe is ready

beats.act: step 1 — the pipeline description for snickerdoodle
beats.narration_text: The first step is running /snickerdoodle in Gru. /snickerdoodle takes a plain-language pipeline description — three to five sentences — and outputs a Claude Code agent prompt you can paste directly into Claude Code to audit your recipe. The description is not a copy-paste of the recipe executive summary. It is a compressed overview that includes what the pipeline does, what data it processes, where data flows, and what the human conductor's role is. Gru uses this description to generate a targeted audit prompt. A vague description — 'my pipeline collects data and analyzes it' — produces a generic audit that misses your specific TODOs. A specific description — 'my pipeline pulls news articles from three Tier-1 sources daily, passes them through a sentiment classification AI node, and produces a ranked digest for a marketing analyst to review before it is sent' — produces an audit that catches the gaps in your specific recipe. The /snickerdoodle description is the input that determines the quality of the audit. Write it before you run the command.

props.artifactHeading: 3–5 sentences before you run the command
artifactLines: /snickerdoodle input: 3–5 sentence pipeline description → outputs a Claude Code agent prompt.
Describe: what it does · what data · where it flows · what the conductor's role is.
Vague description → generic audit → misses your specific TODOs.
Specific description → targeted audit → catches the gaps in your recipe.
Write the description before you open Gru — input quality determines audit quality.
props.sparkLine: Write the description first. Audit quality depends on it.

beats.narration_text: Step two: paste the Claude Code agent prompt from Gru into Claude Code. Run it against your recipe file. The agent reads the recipe and adds typed TODO items for anything it identifies as unresolved, ambiguous, or missing. These are additional annotations on top of the TODOs you wrote in Episode 4 — the agent may catch things you missed. For each TODO the agent adds, it will use one of the five types: DATA SOURCE, DEFINE, DEV, APPROVE, or REPORT FIELD. Document everything — the exact description you gave /snickerdoodle, every TODO the agent generated including its type and the description, which TODOs you resolved and how, and which TODOs remain open with their real blockers. The documentation of this run is what gets submitted in the assignment. A section that says 'I ran /snickerdoodle and it found some issues' is not documentation. The documentation names every TODO generated, every resolution, and every open blocker.

props.artifactHeading: What to document from the run
artifactLines: Paste Claude Code agent prompt from Gru into Claude Code → agent annotates your recipe.
Agent adds typed TODOs for unresolved/ambiguous/missing items — on top of your E4 TODOs.
Document: exact /snickerdoodle description used · every TODO generated (type + description).
Resolved TODOs: what decision was made.
Open TODOs: real blocker (not 'haven't decided').
'I ran it and found some issues' is not documentation — name every TODO.
props.sparkLine: Every generated TODO. Every resolution. Every open blocker.

beats.narration_text: Step three: close as many TODOs as you can before submitting. A TODO is closed when the decision is made, the implementation exists, or the approval has been granted. For each closed TODO, write a one-sentence resolution that names what was decided or built. Then run /claude in Gru — verify the exact command name in the current Gru reference at build time. /claude reviews the annotated recipe and confirms it is ready for use. If Gru flags remaining issues, address them or document them as open with real blockers. Open TODOs with real blockers are acceptable — 'waiting on DEV implementation for the news API connector' is a legitimate open item. Open TODOs with no blockers signal deferred decisions the student has not made. The assignment grader can tell the difference between 'this is blocked by something outside my control' and 'I haven't thought about this yet.' Write the distinction clearly for each open item.

props.artifactHeading: Close what you can. Document the rest.
artifactLines: Closed TODO: decision made / implementation exists / approval granted → one-sentence resolution.
Run /claude in Gru (verify current command name at build time) → confirms recipe is ready.
Gru flags issues → address them or document as open with real blockers.
Acceptable open: 'Waiting on DEV for news API connector' — external blocker.
Not acceptable: open TODO with no blocker. Write the distinction for each open item.
props.sparkLine: Close what you can. For the rest: real blocker or a decision made.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Run /snickerdoodle. Three steps: write a three-to-five sentence pipeline description that is specific enough to produce a targeted audit, then run /snickerdoodle in Gru to get the Claude Code agent prompt, then paste that prompt into Claude Code to annotate the recipe. Document everything: the description you used, every TODO the agent generated with its type and description, which TODOs you resolved and how, and which remain open with real blockers. Run /claude in Gru when TODOs are closed to confirm readiness. A missing /snickerdoodle section, or a section that describes the run without documenting the TODOs, costs Part 4 points. Open TODOs with real external blockers are acceptable. Open TODOs with no blockers are not. Episode 6 assembles the submission.

props.artifactTitle: Run /snickerdoodle: Audit and Close Your TODOs
artifactLines: 3 steps: specific description → /snickerdoodle → Claude Code annotates → /claude confirms.
Document: description used · every TODO (type + text) · resolutions · open blockers.
Missing section or undocumented TODOs = Part 4 points lost.
Open TODO + real external blocker = acceptable.
Open TODO + no blocker = deferred decision. Write the distinction.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Before you run /snickerdoodle, use this prompt to write the description it needs. Paste this into Claude — fill in the brackets from your recipe and config. The prompt reads: I need to write a three-to-five sentence pipeline description to input into a recipe-auditing tool. The description should be specific enough to produce a targeted audit of my Madison tool recipe. Here is what my tool does: [paste the executive summary from your recipe]. It has these data sources: [list source names from the config]. The AI step does this: [one sentence from your recipe step 3]. The primary user is: [role and situation]. The conductor's hardest judgment call is: [one sentence naming the judgment type and when it fires]. Write a three-to-five sentence pipeline description that includes all of these elements and is specific enough for an audit tool to identify gaps in a recipe for this exact pipeline. After Claude gives you the description, read it out loud. If any sentence sounds vague or could apply to a different kind of pipeline, rewrite it to be more specific to yours. That description is what goes into /snickerdoodle.

props.segment: Run /snickerdoodle: Audit and Close Your TODOs
props.command: I need a 3–5 sentence /snickerdoodle description specific enough for a recipe audit. Tool: [exec summary]. Sources: [list]. AI step: [one sentence]. Primary user: [role + situation]. Hardest judgment call: [type + when it fires]. Write the description — specific enough that an audit tool can identify gaps in this exact pipeline.
props.runningText: read the description aloud — if it could apply to a different pipeline, rewrite it to be more specific…

beats.narration_text: Run /snickerdoodle: Audit and Close Your TODOs. Liam, in for Bear.
